_schemaNote: Extends schemas/outcome-manifest.schema.json: each requiredEdge carries partition ∈ {intra-feature(=library-covered), seam(=inter-feature)}. requirements carry `feature`. Authored blind to archetype internals; HASH-PIN before any run.

statedOutcomes.statement: a person can sign in through their company's SSO identity provider, stay signed in while using Hearth, and sign out
an org owner or admin can invite a teammate by email, and that person can accept or decline the invite
owners and admins can do org- and people-management things that ordinary members cannot, and the product enforces that distinction wherever it applies
a member can create a task in a project, assign it, edit it, and mark it complete
a person can attach a file to a task and download it again later
the org has an audit trail of who-did-what that the people running it can review for compliance
the right people are notified, in-app and by email, when things they care about change, and can see a feed of recent activity
an organization can subscribe and pay per seat, and how many people it can have follows from what it is paying for

features.name: Sign in with company SSO; stay signed in; sign out
Organizations and teams; invite teammates by email; accept/decline invites
Roles and permissions: owners/admins/members see and do different things
Projects and tasks: members create, assign, edit, and complete tasks within a project
Attach files to tasks; download them later
An audit log of who did what, for compliance
Activity feed + email/in-app notifications when things change
Paid subscription billing per organization seat

requirements.description: per-org SSO/OIDC provider configuration: issuer, client id/secret, scopes, allowed redirect/callback URL — each customer company brings its own IdP, so config is keyed by org
initiate-login route that redirects the browser to the org's IdP authorize endpoint with state + nonce
redirect/callback route that exchanges the code for tokens, validates state/nonce, resolves the identity, and establishes a session
validate ID token signature/issuer/audience/expiry against the org IdP's JWKS before trusting any claim
persisted identity/user record keyed by (org, IdP subject), created/linked on first successful login
server-side session store that issues a session on login and is looked up on each request
middleware that reads the session cookie each request and attaches the authenticated identity, so users stay signed in
logout route that destroys the session, clears the cookie, optionally hits the IdP end-session endpoint
auth failure paths: IdP error, denied consent, state mismatch, expired/invalid token, callback with no pending login, unknown-org IdP
organization record — the top-level tenant a company owns; everything (teams, projects, billing, audit) hangs off an org
team records nested under an org; people are organized into teams within the org
membership join record linking an identity to an org/team with a role; the roster of who has actually joined
pending-invite record: invitee email, org/team, inviting actor, token, status (pending/accepted/declined/expired)
create-and-send an invite by email — only an owner/admin may invite; mints a single-use invite token
accept an invite: redeem the token, create the membership, mark the invite accepted
decline an invite: mark it declined, no membership created
the outbound email carrying the invite link/token to the invitee's address
the role taxonomy (owner, admin, member) and the permission grants each role carries
a reusable can(actor, action, resource) predicate resolving a membership's role to an allow/deny
route/middleware guard that enforces permission-check on every protected action and rejects unauthorized requests
owner/admin-only operation to change a member's role; the sensitive org/people-management surface
project record scoped to an org/team; the container a team works in
task record nested under a project: title/body, status (open/done), assignee, timestamps
create a task within a project
assign a task to a person (a member of the org)
edit a task's fields
mark a task complete — move it from open to done
file-metadata record: name, size, content-type, owner, the task it is attached to, and a pointer to the blob
the object/blob store holding file bytes (e.g. bucket); upload puts bytes, download fetches them
upload a file and attach it to a task: accept bytes, store the blob, write file metadata linked to the task
download a previously attached file: authorize, fetch the blob, stream it back
append-only audit record: actor, action, target, org, timestamp; durable and tamper-evident
the audit-emit hook that records a compliance-relevant event when it happens; called by the producing actions
owner/admin-only review surface to query/read the audit trail when an auditor asks
per-recipient notification record: event ref, recipient, read/unread, channel-delivery status
the typed change-events the system emits (task-assigned, invite-accepted, comment, project-update) that drive notifications and the feed
resolve who-cares for an event and fan it out to their channels (in-app + email)
the email delivery channel for notifications (and the shared outbound-email transport)
the in-app notification channel/inbox the recipient reads
a feed view of recent activity in the workspace, derived from the event stream
per-org subscription record: plan, seat count purchased, status, billing-provider customer/subscription ids
the catalog of plans and per-seat pricing the org chooses from
integration with the external payment/billing provider: create customer, subscribe, charge per seat
inbound webhook from the billing provider reconciling subscription/payment state back into the subscription store
the live count of occupied seats (joined memberships) measured against seats purchased
gate that prevents adding a member beyond purchased seats; growing/shrinking the team is reflected in what is billed

mustHaves.statement: a person can sign in through their company's SSO
a signed-in person stays signed in while using Hearth
a person can sign out and the session ends
an owner/admin can invite a teammate by email
an invited person can accept and join
owners/admins can do org/people-management things members cannot, enforced product-side
a member can create a task in a project
a member can assign a task
a member can edit a task
a member can mark a task complete
a person can attach a file to a task
a person can download an attached file later
the org has a reviewable audit trail of who-did-what for compliance
the right people are notified in-app and by email when things change
people can see a feed of recent activity
an org can subscribe and pay per seat
how many people the org can have follows from what it is paying for

requiredEdges.why: the login redirect needs the org IdP's authorize URL, client id, and scopes
the code-for-token exchange needs the org IdP client id/secret and token endpoint
the callback completes the flow login started and verifies the state it issued
the callback must validate the returned ID token before establishing a session
validation needs the org IdP issuer, audience, and JWKS endpoint
a successful callback links/creates the identity record
a successful callback establishes a session
a session references the persisted identity it belongs to
middleware looks the session up on each request
logout destroys the session in the store
the auth error states are the failure branches of the callback flow
teams are nested under an org
a membership joins an identity to an org
a membership may scope to a team within the org
an invite is into a specific org/team
sending an invite writes a pending invite record
sending an invite delivers the invite link by email
accept redeems the pending invite token and marks it accepted
accepting an invite creates the membership (the roster reflects who joined)
decline marks the pending invite declined
the predicate resolves a role's grants from the role taxonomy
the guard calls the permission predicate to allow/deny
changing a role writes a value from the role taxonomy
tasks are nested under a project
create writes a task row
a task is created within an existing project
assign updates the task's assignee
edit updates task fields
complete flips the task status to done
upload writes file metadata
upload puts the bytes into blob storage
download looks up the file metadata/blob pointer
download fetches the bytes from blob storage
the emit hook appends to the audit store
review reads/queries the audit store
dispatch consumes the typed change-events
dispatch writes a per-recipient notification record
dispatch fans out to the email channel
dispatch fans out to the in-app channel
the in-app inbox reads the recipient's notification records
the feed is derived from the event stream
a subscription references the chosen plan from the catalog
subscribing/charging reads and writes the org subscription record
the provider integration charges per-seat pricing from the plan catalog
the webhook reconciles provider state back into the subscription record
the counter compares occupied seats against seats purchased on the subscription
enforcement reads the live seat count to decide whether another member fits
SEAM: every identity belongs to exactly one tenant org; the IdP-resolved identity must hang off the org it signed into. SSO and org-modeling are separate features but multi-tenancy welds them.
SEAM: SSO config is per-org (each company brings its own IdP), so the provider-config keys off the org record — config lives in the SSO feature, the org in another.

SEAM: to authorize anything, the request context needs not just identity (SSO) but the actor's membership+role in the active org (membership feature) — the join the auth middleware silently depends on.
SEAM: a permission decision resolves the actor's ROLE from their membership; RBAC's role lives on the membership record owned by the org/membership feature, not inside RBAC.
SEAM: the role enum is stored ON the membership; the membership feature persists the field the role-model defines.
SEAM: changing a role mutates the membership record (owned by the org/membership feature) from the RBAC feature.
SEAM: authorization runs only after authentication has attached the identity — the RBAC guard chains onto the SSO session middleware.
SEAM: only owners/admins may invite — the invite send path must pass the RBAC guard. The role gate (RBAC feature) constrains the invite action (membership feature).
SEAM: changing a member's role is owner/admin-only; the role-management action is gated by the RBAC guard.
SEAM: accepting an invite occupies a seat — it must be blocked if the org is at its purchased seat limit. The team size 'follows from what they are paying for' links invites (membership) to billing.
SEAM: occupied seats = count of joined memberships; the billing seat-counter reads the membership roster owned by another feature.
SEAM: removing a member (shrinking the team) must update the billed seat count — letting people go is reflected in billing.
SEAM: a project is owned by an org/team (tenancy); projects feature hangs off the org feature.
SEAM: a task can only be assigned to someone who is a member of the org — assignment (tasks feature) validates against the roster (membership feature).
SEAM: task mutations run behind the authorization guard (members may, but the route still enforces auth + tenancy) — the RBAC enforcement applies to the tasks feature.
SEAM: project/task routes require an authenticated session; the tasks feature depends on the SSO auth context.
SEAM: a file is attached to a task — the file feature references the task record owned by the projects/tasks feature.
SEAM: download must authorize the requester against the task/org the file belongs to — file access control routes through the RBAC/tenancy guard.
SEAM: upload requires an authenticated actor (the file's owner) from the SSO session context.
SEAM: joining/leaving is a compliance event — invite-accept must emit an audit entry. Audit feature observes the membership feature.
SEAM: role changes are compliance-relevant — role-management emits an audit entry. Audit observes RBAC.
SEAM: changes to work are audited — task edits emit audit entries. Audit observes the tasks feature.
SEAM: completing work is a recorded change — emits an audit entry. Audit observes the tasks feature.
SEAM: access to files is explicitly named as audited — download emits an audit entry. Audit observes the files feature.
SEAM: file changes are audited — upload emits an audit entry. Audit observes the files feature.
SEAM: billing events are explicitly named as audited — subscription/seat changes emit audit entries. Audit observes the billing feature.
SEAM: the audit entry records the actor's role/context; audit-write reads RBAC context to attribute the event.
SEAM: the audit-review surface is owner/admin-only — reviewing the trail is gated by the RBAC guard.
SEAM: 'a task assigned' is a named notification trigger — task-assign emits a notification event. Notifications observe the tasks feature.
SEAM: 'an invite accepted' is a named notification trigger — invite-accept emits an event. Notifications observe the membership feature.
SEAM: 'a project update / comment / change' triggers notifications — task changes emit events. Notifications observe the tasks feature.
SEAM: dispatch must resolve WHO cares about an event from the org roster/membership — the recipient set comes from the membership feature.
SEAM: notification fan-out must not leak an event to someone not entitled to see the underlying resource — dispatch consults RBAC before notifying.
SEAM: invite emails and notification emails share one outbound-email transport — the two features must converge on a single email-sending surface rather than each building its own.
SEAM: inviting toward a full org should be reflected against purchased seats — invite send (membership) consults the billing seat count before consuming capacity.
SEAM: a subscription belongs to an org (tenancy); billing hangs off the org feature.
SEAM: choosing a plan / paying is owner-only — the billing action is gated by the RBAC guard.
